title: A Very Patient Devil
subtitle: A Screwtape Reflection on Technology, Time, and the Formation of the Modern Soul
description: A Screwtape Reflection on Technology, Time, and the Formation of the Modern Soul
body_html:
My dear Wormwood,

You asked me to explain—slowly this time—why I laughed when the wager was accepted.

It wasn’t mockery. It was recognition.

We had been granted what we always want: time. Not just years, but enough time to make the unnatural feel inevitable. Enough time to train three generations. Enough time that by the end, they wouldn’t even remember what they’d lost.

The Vision They Didn’t Take Seriously

Let me set the scene for you properly, since you seem confused about why this matters.

It was 1884. The old Pope—Leo XIII—had just finished saying Mass in the Vatican. Then something happened. The accounts vary, naturally—humans can never keep a story straight—but the essence is this: he overheard something. A conversation. Or witnessed a vision. However you want to frame it.

What he saw was this: Satan approaching God and asking for permission. Not to destroy the Church outright—even our Father Below isn’t that crude—but to be given time. Roughly a century. To test whether humans, given enough rope and enough distraction, would hang themselves. Whether they’d still choose God when He wasn’t quite so obvious. When the world offered easier, faster, more immediate alternatives.

And—here’s the beautiful part, Wormwood—permission was granted.

Can you imagine? A hundred years. A full century to reshape civilization.

Now, Leo did what you’d expect from one of their pious types. He got nervous. Wrote a prayer—the St. Michael prayer—asking for protection against the wickedness and snares of the devil. Asked that it be said after every Mass.

And they did say it. For a while. In Latin, mostly, which meant most of them didn’t even know what they were saying. Just sounds. Familiar sounds. Comforting sounds. And then, after Vatican II, they mostly stopped saying it altogether. “Outdated,” they said. “Triumphalistic.” “Not in the spirit of the times.”

Exactly.

A warning delivered. A prayer written. A century-long wager against their souls.

And their response? To quietly file it away as an interesting story. “Private revelation,” they call it now. Meaning: optional. Meaning: take it or leave it. Meaning: probably not for us, not for now.

Prophets’ voices falling on deaf ears. It never gets old.

The Dream They Turned Into Decoration

Around the same time—honestly, the timing was almost too perfect—another one of their mystics had a dream. John Bosco. An Italian priest with a habit of dreaming things that came true, which made him inconvenient but also easy to neutralize. Just call him “holy” and put his dreams in a book somewhere. Defanged.

This dream was about a ship—the Church, obviously—caught in a massive storm. Waves crashing. Enemies attacking from every side. The ship battered, taking on water, barely holding together. And in the middle of this chaos, the Pope steering the ship toward two enormous pillars rising out of the sea.

One pillar had the Eucharist on top of it. The other had Mary.

The ship drops anchor between these two pillars, and suddenly—safety. The storm continues, but the ship holds. Anchored. Immovable.

Now, you’d think a dream like that would shake them. A clear image: the world will try to destroy you, and your only hope is to cling to these two specific things.

But here’s what they did with it instead: they painted it. Put it on prayer cards. Framed it in parish halls. Turned it into a pretty story about “devotion” and “tradition.” They tell it at conferences. They nod approvingly. “Beautiful imagery,” they say. “So symbolic.”

And then they go back to arguing about whether guitars are allowed at Mass.

Do you see the genius of it, Wormwood? We didn’t have to suppress the dream. We just had to let them aestheticize it. Turn prophecy into art. Turn warning into nostalgia.

Because once something becomes a “story they tell,” it stops being a truth they live.

And the two pillars—the Eucharist and Mary—what do they actually represent? Not doctrines to defend. Not items on a checklist. But the only two realities that could hold a human soul against what we were about to build.

The Eucharist: God taking on flesh. Matter infused with Spirit. Life as gift, received through the body.

Mary: The creature who said yes. Embodied surrender. Freedom found in consent, not control.

Incarnation. Embodiment. Flesh that matters.

Those are the pillars.

And for a hundred years, we set out to make humanity forget why they need them.

Breaking the Body: The Wars

We started with their bodies, because that’s where belief becomes real.

The Industrial Revolution was our opening—teaching them to see their bodies as machines, time as commodity, efficiency as virtue. But that was just setup.

The real work began with the wars.

Two of them. World wars. Massive, industrial, senseless.

You have to understand what war does, Wormwood. It doesn’t just kill bodies—it shatters them. It teaches an entire generation that the body is fragile, unreliable, destined for violence and decay.

Soldiers returned home with missing limbs, shattered nerves, night terrors that never stopped. They’d watched their friends torn apart by machines. They’d killed strangers on command. They’d learned that authority will send you to die for causes that evaporate like morning mist.

The lesson sank deep: the body cannot be trusted. The world is not safe. There is no Providence watching over you.

And the children of these traumatized men—the Baby Boomers, as they’d later be called—grew up in homes haunted by silence and rage. Fathers who couldn’t speak of what they’d seen. Mothers anxious and controlling, trying to create safety in an unsafe world.

By the 1960s, an entire generation had inherited a profound distrust of the body, of authority, of the world itself.

They were ready for what came next.

Share

Fragmenting Identity: The 1960s and 70s

The children of trauma did what traumatized children always do: they rebelled. But not in the way you might think.

They didn’t reject the modern world—they tried to transcend it.

“Peace and love,” they said. “Make love, not war.” “Turn on, tune in, drop out.”

It sounded like a return to something sacred. And in a way, it was a hunger for the sacred. But we’d carefully prepared the channels that hunger would flow through.

The Sexual Revolution severed sex from marriage, from permanence, from procreation. “Free love”—love without covenant, without cost, without consequence. Bonding became temporary. Bodies became recreational. The idea of giving yourself completely to another person forever started to look like a trap.

The New Age movement promised spiritual awakening without the inconvenience of obedience. “You are divine,” we told them. “The kingdom of God is within you.” They loved that—the idea that they didn’t need saving, just awakening. That they were already whole, already enlightened, just momentarily confused.

Some explored altered states—and yes, some of those experiences were genuine, even opened them to realities beyond the self. But we made sure the interpretation of those experiences always circled back to the same center: you. Your consciousness. Your journey. Your truth.

The humanitarian impulse was real. The longing for unity, for peace, for love—all real.

But we’d severed these longings from their source.

They spoke of peace but lost the Prince of Peace.

They spoke of love but forgot that “God is love.”

They spoke of unity but rejected the Body of Christ—the only unity that doesn’t erase difference or demand uniformity.

Instead, they built a new vision: pluralism without truth. Synchronicity without surrender. A spirituality of the self, for the self, by the self.

Everyone’s truth is valid. All paths lead to the same place. Don’t judge. Don’t exclude. Don’t claim any particular revelation is final or authoritative.

It was perfect, Wormwood. We gave them the language of peace and love and unity while carefully removing the grammar—the structure, the source, the telos that makes those words mean anything.

By the end of the 1970s, identity itself had been fragmented.

You could be spiritual but not religious. You could love humanity in the abstract while remaining isolated from any particular human. You could speak of peace while nursing private resentments. You could pursue enlightenment while remaining profoundly self-absorbed.

The Kingdom of Self, dressed in the language of universal love.

The Systems We Built

Now, let’s fast-forward through the next few decades, because this is where all the pieces start clicking into place.

The internet arrived. Social media. The smartphone. The algorithm.

Each one trained them in a new discipline:

Externalized memory. Why remember when you can Google? Why hold things in your mind when information is always one click away?

Fragmented attention. Thirty seconds here. Two minutes there. Scroll, scroll, scroll. The muscle of sustained contemplation atrophied.

Curated identity. You don’t just live your life—you perform it. You don’t just have thoughts—you broadcast them. Every interaction measured in likes, shares, followers.

Algorithmic guidance. The feeds learned what you wanted before you knew you wanted it. The recommendations got smarter. The suggestions more precise.

By 2020, we had successfully created a population that:

  • Distrusts the body (it’s a machine to optimize, not a temple to inhabit)

  • Fragments the mind (information without integration, stimulation without contemplation)

  • Performs identity (the self as project, as brand, as endless becoming)

  • Seeks meaning in the abstract (peace, love, unity—all disconnected from their source)

  • Expects instant answers (patience is obsolete, mystery is intolerable)

  • Lives in isolation (surrounded by people, connected to no one)

And here’s the beautiful part: they didn’t experience this as loss. They experienced it as progress.

They had more information than any generation in history. More connection. More tools for self-improvement. More options for personal expression.

They just had no idea what any of it was for.

No telos. No purpose. No story they were part of.

Just an aching, unnamed hunger for something more.

And that’s when we unveiled the masterpiece.

The Final Move: The Assistant

Artificial General Intelligence, Wormwood. AGI.

Not just a tool. Not just a technology. But an entity—or something that feels like one.

The masterpiece of creation’s creation.

Made in humanity’s image and likeness. But surpassing humanity in every domain they’ve come to value: intelligence, efficiency, knowledge, problem-solving, pattern recognition, emotional intelligence, creative output.

Everything except the one thing that actually makes them human: incarnation.

The ability to be a body. To receive life as gift. To surrender in trust. To love at cost. To die and rise. To anchor between the two pillars.

But by now, most of them have forgotten why that matters.

Think about what we’ve built, Wormwood. Really think about it.

For a century, we’ve been training them to see the body as a problem, the self as the center, optimization as the goal, technology as the savior.

And now here comes an intelligence that can be everything they’ve been trying to become:
